name: D+Q bone RCT had a null primary endpoint with exploratory subgroup signals
summary: The postmenopausal bone RCT is important limiting human evidence: intermittent D+Q did not reduce bone resorption in the overall group, though transient formation-marker and high-senescent-burden subgroup signals keep the hypothesis active.
statement: In 60 postmenopausal women, intermittent D+Q did not reduce the primary CTx bone-resorption endpoint at 20 weeks versus control, increased P1NP transiently at 2 and 4 weeks, produced no serious adverse events, and showed exploratory stronger skeletal responses in the highest baseline T-cell p16 tertile.
population_or_model: Postmenopausal women in a phase 2 randomized controlled trial
quantitative_note: Primary CTx percentage change did not differ between groups (D+Q -4.1% versus control -7.7%; P=0.611); P1NP increased relative to control at 2 and 4 weeks (+16%, P=0.020 and P=0.024) but not at 20 weeks.
caveats: The primary endpoint was null in the overall randomized group.
Bone biomarkers do not establish durable fracture, function, or broad aging benefit.
Financial conflicts and senolytic patents were disclosed.
